title: Engineering the Features
concept: Feature engineering transforms cleaned raw columns — like a signup date or a plan name — into new numeric inputs, such as tenure in months or one-hot encoded categories, that carry more predictive signal for the model.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In StreamBox's data, why was a watch_hours value of 9,999 a problem?

options.text: It's physically impossible in a 30-day month (max 720 hours), making it an outlier

exercises.explain: Right! A 30-day month has at most 720 hours, so 9,999 is an outlier that needed cleaning.

cards.title: A date the model can't read
cards.body: StreamBox's data is clean now, but signup_date still just says "2023-01-15". A model can't do math with a date string — it needs a number that actually says something about churn risk, like how long this customer has stuck around.

cards.title: Feature engineering
cards.body: Feature engineering means transforming cleaned raw columns into new inputs — called features — that carry more predictive signal than the raw column did on its own.

cards.title: Batter, not flour and eggs
cards.body: An oven can't bake with a bag of flour and a carton of eggs sitting side by side — they have to be mixed into batter first. Feature engineering is that mixing step: raw columns become the actual "batter" the model can work with.

cards.title: Three transforms for StreamBox
cards.body: Signup date 2023-01-15 becomes tenure_months: 18 (measured against today). Plan type "Standard" becomes three one-hot columns: is_basic: 0, is_standard: 1, is_premium: 0. And watch_hours (0–720) gets scaled down to a 0–1 range so no single feature overpowers the others.

cards.title: Having the column isn't the same as using it
cards.body: You might think that since signup_date is already in the dataset, the model "already knows" how long someone's been a customer. In reality, a raw date string is meaningless to a model — it has to be converted into a number like tenure_months before it carries any signal.

exercises.prompt: Why does StreamBox convert plan_type into one-hot encoded columns instead of feeding the model the word "Standard" directly?

options.text: Models need numeric inputs, and one-hot encoding turns each category into its own binary (0/1) column
It removes the need for cleaning
It changes plan_type into the target variable

exercises.explain: Correct! One-hot encoding converts a text category into numeric 0/1 columns the model can actually use.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Scaling watch_hours down to a 0–1 range changes what the number means, not just its scale.
exercises.explain: False! Scaling keeps the relative differences between customers — it just puts the numbers on a comparable range so one feature doesn't dominate.

exercises.sentence: Converting signup_date into a number of months since signup creates a new feature called ___.
exercises.explain: tenure_months turns a raw date into a number that directly signals how long someone has been a customer.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a properly engineered feature, ready for a model?
exercises.explain: Correct! tenure_months is a clean number derived from the raw date — exactly what a model needs.

exercises.prompt: A StreamBox customer signed up on 2023-01-15 (today is 2024-07-15), is on the "Premium" plan, and watched 340 of a possible 720 hours last month. Which set of engineered features correctly represents this customer for the model?
exercises.explain: Exactly! 18 months of tenure, a one-hot flag for Premium, and watch hours scaled to roughly 0.47 (340/720) are all properly engineered numeric features. Next: it's finally time to pick a model and train it!
